title: Albóndigas en salsa
subtitle: Spanish meatballs in tomato sauce
excerpt: Pork and beef meatballs simmered in a smoky tomato sauce with paprika, bay, and a splash of wine. The Spanish version of a dish that appears across Southern Europe — more paprika, less tomato than the Italian equivalent. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Albóndigas en salsa is one of the classic tapas and main-course dishes of Spain, with regional variations across Catalonia, Andalusia, and Castile. Documented by Penelope Casas in Tapas: The Little Dishes of Spain (1985) and Claudia Roden in The Food of Spain (2011). The sauce typically uses smoked paprika and sometimes a splash of dry sherry or white wine.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked meatballs in sauce for up to three months. Defrost overnight and reheat gently.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. The sauce improves with time; make it a day ahead if possible.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sauce can be made a day ahead. Roll and refrigerate the meatballs up to 12 hours before cooking.

content.text: The key to albóndigas that hold together is keeping the mix cold before rolling and not overworking the meat. Warm hands break down the fat in the mince, which makes the meatballs dense and prone to falling apart in the sauce. Mix quickly, refrigerate for 15 minutes if the kitchen is warm, then roll fast.

The sauce here is built on onion, smoked paprika, and tinned tomatoes — simpler than a long-cooked ragù, thicker and more paprika-forward than most Italian tomato sauces. The meatballs finish cooking in the sauce, which flavours both components as they simmer together.

Combine {{pork-mince}} of pork mince and {{beef-mince}} of beef mince in a large bowl with {{garlic}} of grated garlic, {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs, the beaten egg, {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Mix briefly with your hands until just combined — overworking makes the meatballs dense. Roll into balls about 3 cm across. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide saucepan over medium-high heat. Brown the meatballs in batches, turning so they colour on all sides, about 3–4 minutes per batch. Remove and set aside.

In the same pan, add {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion over medium heat for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée; cook for 1 minute. Add {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika and stir for 30 seconds. Pour in the wine if using and let it bubble until absorbed. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of hand-crushed tomatoes, {{bay-leaves}} bay leaves, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Simmer for 10 minutes. Return the meatballs to the pan, nestling them into the sauce. Cover and simmer for 15 minutes until cooked through. Remove the bay leaves and serve.

items.symptom: Meatballs falling apart in the sauce
items.cause: Mix not bound enough, or meatballs not browned first to form a crust.
items.fix: Make sure the egg and breadcrumbs are well distributed before rolling. Brown the meatballs fully before they go into the sauce — the crust holds them together.

items.cause: Meat overworked when mixing, or cooked too long.
items.fix: Mix only until combined. The meatballs need 15 minutes simmering in sauce, not longer.

items.cause: Tomatoes not cooked long enough to lose their rawness.
items.fix: Simmer the sauce for at least 10 minutes before adding the meatballs. If still sharp, add a pinch of sugar.

content.text: Albóndigas appear across the Spanish-speaking world, from the tapas bars of Madrid to the home cooking of Mexico, where the sauce changes character entirely. In Spain the dish is served as a tapa in small portions or as a main course with bread and wine. The smoked paprika in the sauce marks it as Spanish rather than Italian; the combination of pork and beef in the mix gives a richer flavour than either mince alone. It is the kind of dish that is better the second day, once the meatballs have had time to absorb the sauce.
